select_when: Choices involve asymmetric payoffs and intuitive judgment is likely to overweight vivid outcomes rather than weighted outcomes.
The team can estimate probabilities and consequences well enough to compare options on a common decision basis.
A decision must trade off upside, downside, and likelihood instead of reacting to whichever outcome feels most salient.
avoid_when: EV math is being used to launder weak assumptions, speculative probabilities, or ethically unacceptable downside into a false aura of rationality.
The case is too assumption-fragile or cognitively overloaded for the numbers to support a trustworthy weighted comparison.

curation_notes.summary: Normalized the model around weighted payoff comparison and rejected donor business-case framing that overstated output specificity.
donor_drops: Dropped donor examples tied to particular industries because the raw source already captures the structural weighted-outcome pattern.
Dropped donor output framing that implied a richer document artifact than the raw model supports.
open_questions: The model works best when probabilities are good enough for comparison, but the exact threshold for 'good enough' remains case-dependent.

select_when.note: The raw file explicitly states when expected-value thinking is useful; donor drafts were narrowed into fewer structural triggers.

avoid_when.note: The first condition is explicit in raw form and the second is a conservative normalization from the raw cognitive-load and false-precision limitations.

input_type.note: The raw file clearly operates on choices with probability-weighted payoffs, but the compact input label is a reviewed normalization.

output_type.note: The raw model compares weighted outcomes rather than producing a full business-case artifact; the output label was narrowed accordingly.

reasoning_types.note: The model is clearly probabilistic and deductive in use, but the taxonomy labels are still reviewed normalization.
